answer:
Let $N$ be the number of students who took the test.
Each student attempted exactly 14 out of the 15 questions.
There are 9 objective questions and 6 fill-in-the-blanks questions.
Let $a_{ij}$ be the number of students who attempted both questions $O_i$ and $F_j$.
We are given that $\sum_{i=1}^9 \sum_{j=1}^6 a_{ij} = 972$.

Since each student attempted 14 questions, the number of questions attempted by all students is $14N$.
The total number of questions is $9 + 6 = 15$.
The number of questions attempted by all students is also the sum of the number of students who attempted each question.
Let $n_i$ be the number of students who attempted question $O_i$.
Let $m_j$ be the number of students who attempted question $F_j$.
Then $n_i + m_j = a_{ij}$ for each $i$ and $j$.
The total number of questions attempted is $\sum_{i=1}^9 n_i + \sum_{j=1}^6 m_j = 14N$.
We are given that $\sum_{i=1}^9 \sum_{j=1}^6 a_{ij} = 972$.
We can rewrite this as $\sum_{i=1}^9 \sum_{j=1}^6 n_i = 972$.
Since each student attempted 14 questions, the number of students who attempted question $O_i$ is $n_i = \sum_{j=1}^6 a_{ij}$.
Therefore, $\sum_{i=1}^9 n_i = \sum_{i=1}^9 \sum_{j=1}^6 a_{ij} = 972$.
Also, $\sum_{j=1}^6 m_j = \sum_{j=1}^6 \sum_{i=1}^9 a_{ij} = \sum_{j=1}^6 \sum_{i=1}^9 a_{ij}$.
We know that $\sum_{i=1}^9 \sum_{j=1}^6 a_{ij} = 972$.
Since each student attempted 14 questions, the total number of attempts is $14N$.
The total number of attempts on objective questions is $\sum_{i=1}^9 n_i = 14N$.
The total number of attempts on fill-in-the-blanks questions is $\sum_{j=1}^6 m_j = 14N$.
We have $\sum_{i=1}^9 n_i = 972$.
Therefore, $14N = 972$.
$N = \frac{972}{14} = \frac{486}{7}$, which is not an integer.

Let $x_i$ be the number of students who attempted question $O_i$.
Let $y_j$ be the number of students who attempted question $F_j$.
We are given that each student attempted 14 questions.
So, the total number of questions attempted is $14N$.
The total number of questions is $9+6=15$.
The number of questions attempted by all students is $14N$.
We have $\sum_{i=1}^9 x_i + \sum_{j=1}^6 y_j = 14N$.

Since $a_{ij}$ is the number of students who attempted both questions $O_i$ and $F_j$, we have $x_i = \sum_{j=1}^6 a_{ij}$ and $y_j = \sum_{i=1}^9 a_{ij}$.
Thus, $\sum_{i=1}^9 \sum_{j=1}^6 a_{ij} = \sum_{i=1}^9 x_i = 972$.
Also, $\sum_{j=1}^6 \sum_{i=1}^9 a_{ij} = \sum_{j=1}^6 y_j$.
We have $14N = \sum_{i=1}^9 x_i + \sum_{j=1}^6 y_j = 972 + \sum_{j=1}^6 y_j$.
Since $x_i = \sum_{j=1}^6 a_{ij}$, we have $x_i \le 6$ for each $i$.
Also, $y_j = \sum_{i=1}^9 a_{ij}$, so $y_j \le 9$ for each $j$.
We have $14N = 972 + \sum_{j=1}^6 y_j$.
Since $y_j \le 9$, $\sum_{j=1}^6 y_j \le 54$.
$14N = 972 + \sum_{j=1}^6 y_j \le 972 + 54 = 1026$.
$N \le \frac{1026}{14} = \frac{513}{7} \approx 73.28$.
Since $N$ is an integer, $N \le 73$.
